title: How to Make Eight GPUs Act Like One: The Art of Hiding Communication
hook: Training a large AI model means splitting one giant matrix multiplication across many GPUs. Then you pay for it: the partial results must be shipped and summed over links roughly 7× slower than each GPU's own memory. In some large-model workloads, GPUs now sit idle more than half the time waiting on those transfers. This paper's fix is not faster wires but smarter scheduling: run the transfer *during* the math. Then total time becomes the maximum of the two, not their sum. Follow one split matrix multiply across 8 H100 GPUs and watch its communication cost drop from 68% of runtime to under 1%.

parts.title: The basic building blocks
parts.lede: We set up one concrete workload — a single matrix multiply split across 8 GPUs — and meet the hardware it runs on. By the end, we see why the split creates a cost that only grows with each GPU generation.

parts.title: The new technology
parts.lede: The paper's core move: overlap the transfer with the math so time is a max, not a sum. Then three design choices — which transfer engine, which scheduling, which library plumbing — decide whether the overlap actually works, and all three compress into eight primitives and one kernel template.

parts.title: Why it beats existing tech
parts.lede: Prior systems each hard-coded one point in this design space. Because ParallelKittens can pick the right mechanism and schedule per workload, it beats hand-tuned specialists and compilers head-to-head on their own benchmarks.

parts.title: Why now
parts.lede: The compute-versus-interconnect gap is widening with every GPU generation, so overlap becomes more valuable, not less. The same 50-line kernels ran unmodified on the next architecture and won again.

scenes.title: Splitting a matrix multiply across GPUs creates a mandatory summation step
scenes.teaches: Tensor parallelism partitions a weight matrix across GPUs, and the resulting partial products must be summed across GPUs — a collective operation that does no useful math.
scenes.caption: Our running example: an 8192×8192×8192 matrix multiply, its weights split 8 ways. Step the split and watch a new obligation appear — 8 partial results, 134 MB each, that must be added together across machines.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A block diagram of \(C = A·W\) with \(A\) (8192×8192 activations) on the left and \(W\) (8192×8192 weights) on top. Buttons step the split from 1 GPU to 8: \(W\) shatters into 8 vertical-color-coded row-slices of size 1024×8192, each landing on a labeled GPU card. Each GPU's output is drawn as a full-size but translucent 8192×8192 grid labeled 'partial product — must be summed with 7 others'. At 8 GPUs, red arrows appear between all cards labeled 'reduce: element-wise sum across GPUs, 134 MB per GPU'. Predict-first prompt before stepping: 'each GPU gets 1/8 of the weights — does each GPU's output also shrink to 1/8?' The reveal: no, each partial output is full-size; the split trades weight storage for a cross-GPU sum.

animatedVariable.name: number of GPUs sharing the weight matrix
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The weight matrix shatters into thinner slices; per-GPU weight memory shrinks, but a full-size 134 MB partial output appears on every card, and red 'sum across GPUs' arrows multiply.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Each partial product is 8192×8192 BF16 values = 134 MB per GPU; with 8 GPUs, 8 such tensors must be summed element-wise.
physicsChecks: Per-GPU weight slice size halves each time GPU count doubles (8192→4096→2048→1024 rows).
Partial output stays 8192×8192 (134 MB) at every split — it never shrinks.
Red inter-GPU arrows exist only when GPU count > 1, and their count grows with GPU count.
At 1 GPU the diagram shows zero communication.

scenes.title: Inside a GPU, every step down the memory hierarchy costs an order of magnitude in bandwidth — and the GPU-to-GPU link is the bottom rung
scenes.teaches: A GPU is ~130 independent processors (SMs) fed by a strict bandwidth pyramid, and the inter-GPU link sits below every internal tier by a wide margin.
scenes.caption: Before the 134 MB sum can hurt us, see where it travels. Sweep down the pyramid: registers move data at 130 TB/s, but the NVLink pipe between GPUs manages 0.45 TB/s — nearly 300× slower.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A pyramid with five labeled tiers, drawn to log-scale width by bandwidth. Top to bottom: per-thread registers (64 KB per SM, ~130 TB/s), per-SM shared memory (227 KB, 33 TB/s), L2 cache (50 MB, 12 TB/s), HBM main memory (80 GB, 3 TB/s), NVLink to peer GPUs (0.45 TB/s per direction). A small inset defines 'SM' at first mention: one of ~132 streaming multiprocessors, each an independent processor running thousands of threads, with the tensor cores (matrix-multiply units) inside. A slider highlights one tier at a time; a stopwatch shows how long our 134 MB partial product takes to cross that tier's bandwidth. Predict-first: 'guess the ratio between the fastest tier and the GPU-to-GPU link before you sweep.'

animatedVariable.name: highlighted memory tier
animatedVariable.range: registers → shared memory → L2 → HBM → NVLink
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The highlighted tier lights up with its capacity and bandwidth, and the stopwatch for moving 134 MB updates: ~1 µs at register bandwidth up to ~298 µs over NVLink.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Registers ~130 TB/s, shared memory 33 TB/s, L2 12 TB/s, HBM 3 TB/s, NVLink 0.45 TB/s — each tier roughly an order of magnitude apart.
physicsChecks: Bandwidth strictly decreases and capacity strictly increases moving down the pyramid.
The 134 MB stopwatch time scales inversely with the selected tier's bandwidth (134 MB ÷ 0.45 TB/s ≈ 298 µs at NVLink).
NVLink is drawn ~6.7× narrower than HBM and ~290× narrower than registers on the log scale.
The SM inset states ~132 SMs per H100 and places tensor cores inside the SM.

scenes.title: Each GPU generation makes the math faster than the pipe, so waiting on transfers eats a growing share of runtime
scenes.teaches: From A100 to B200, tensor-core compute grew 7.2× but NVLink only 3×, so a naive compute-then-communicate kernel spends an ever-larger fraction of its time on communication.
scenes.caption: Step through three GPU generations and watch our matmul's timeline: the green compute segment shrinks 7.2×, the red transfer segment only 3×. Faster hardware makes the waiting problem worse, not better.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Two panels. Top: grouped bar chart of hardware trends across A100 → H100 → B200 — tensor-core throughput (7.2× total growth) vs NVLink bandwidth (3×) vs inter-node links (2×). Bottom: a wall-clock timeline of the naive version of our workload — first the green bar (compute the partial product), then the red bar (ship and sum 134 MB), strictly in sequence. Buttons switch generation; both bars rescale and a percentage readout shows communication's share of total time growing. A callout cites the paper: communication already exceeds 50% of runtime in some large-language-model workloads. Predict-first: 'if compute gets 7× faster and the link 3×, does the red share go up or down?'

animatedVariable.name: GPU generation
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The green compute bar shrinks faster than the red communication bar, and the 'communication share of total time' percentage climbs with each generation.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: A100→B200: tensor-core throughput ×7.2, HBM bandwidth ×5.1, NVLink ×3, inter-node ×2; communication exceeds 50% of runtime in some LLM workloads.
physicsChecks: Communication's percentage share strictly increases from A100 to B200 in the timeline.
The compute bar shrinks by a larger factor than the communication bar across the full sweep (7.2× vs 3×).
The timeline shows compute and communication strictly sequential — no overlap — in this naive baseline.
Total time decreases with each generation even as the red share grows (both bars shrink, red less).

scenes.title: If transfers run during the math, total time is the maximum of the two, not their sum
scenes.teaches: GPUs have separate hardware for computing and for transferring, so a pipelined kernel's cost is \(T = T_{launch} + \max(T_{comp}, T_{mem}, T_{comm}) + T_{non\text{-}overlap} + T_{sync}\) — communication is free whenever it fits under the compute.
scenes.caption: The tensor cores and the transfer engine are separate hardware — they can run at once. Flip the toggle and watch the red bar slide underneath the green one: sum becomes max.
scenes.visualMetaphor: The same green/red timeline from the previous scene, now with two lanes labeled 'tensor cores' and 'transfer engine' to make the separate hardware visible. Toggle OFF (sequential): red follows green in one lane; total = green + red. Toggle ON (pipelined): the output is chopped into tiles, and each tile's red transfer starts the moment its green compute finishes, running in the second lane while the next tile computes — red disappears under green except a sliver at the end. The equation \(T_{kernel} = T_{launch} + \max(T_{comp}, T_{mem}, T_{comm}) + T_{non\text{-}overlap} + T_{sync}\) is displayed with each term's segment color-matched on the timeline. Predict-first: 'toggle on — how short can total time get? Shorter than the green bar alone?' Reveal: never shorter than the max.

animatedVariable.name: pipelining
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The red communication bar slides from after the green compute bar into a parallel lane beneath it; the total-time bracket snaps from green+red down to max(green, red) plus small launch/sync slivers.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: The paper's cost model: \(T_{kernel} = T_{launch} + \max(T_{comp}, T_{mem}, T_{comm}) + T_{non\text{-}overlap} + T_{sync}\).
physicsChecks: Pipelined total time is never shorter than the longer of the two bars (the max is a hard floor).
Toggling on reduces total time by exactly the length of the hidden red portion, never more.
A small non-overlappable sliver (first tile's compute, last tile's transfer) remains visible even when pipelined.
The two lanes are labeled as distinct hardware units, making the cause of simultaneity visible.

scenes.title: One inequality decides whether communication vanishes: \(K \geq sR/2B\)
scenes.teaches: Per output tile, compute time \(2mnK/R\) hides transfer time \(smn/B\) exactly when \(K \geq sR/2B\) — the tile size cancels out, leaving a clean threshold on the inner dimension.
scenes.caption: Drag the inner dimension \(K\) and watch each tile's transfer slide under its compute. For BF16 on H100 the crossover is \(K ≈ 2198\) — and measured non-hidden communication falls from 68% at \(K=512\) to under 1% at \(K=4096\).
scenes.visualMetaphor: Left: one \(m×n\) output tile of our matmul with its two costs annotated — compute \(2mnK/R\) and transfer \(smn/B\) — and the algebra shown: the \(mn\) cancels, giving the condition \(K \geq sR/2B\). Plugging H100 numbers (\(s=2\) bytes, \(R = 989\) TFLOP/s, \(B = 450\) GB/s) yields \(K ≥ 2198\). Right: the two-lane pipelined timeline; a slider on \(K\) stretches the green per-tile compute bars while red transfer bars stay fixed length. Below \(K=2198\), red bars poke out past the green and a 'non-hidden communication %' gauge is large; above it, red fully tucks under green. Measured checkpoints from the paper are pinned on the slider track: 68% at \(K=512\), 26% at \(K=2048\), <1% at \(K=4096\). Predict-first: 'does a bigger tile (larger \(m,n\)) help? Slide tile size — nothing changes; only \(K\) matters.'

animatedVariable.name: inner dimension K
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Green per-tile compute bars lengthen proportionally to K while red transfer bars stay fixed; the non-hidden-communication gauge collapses toward 0% as K crosses 2198.

physicsChecks: Compute bar length scales linearly with K; transfer bar length is independent of K.
The gauge crosses from >0% to ≈0% at K ≈ 2198, matching the displayed formula.
Changing the tile dimensions m, n rescales both bars identically and leaves the crossover K unchanged (the mn cancellation is visible).
Gauge readings at K = 512, 2048, 4096 match the paper's 68%, 26%, <1% pins within the plotted markers.

scenes.title: There are three ways to push bytes over NVLink, and the highest-bandwidth one is useless for small messages
scenes.teaches: The copy engine, the TMA, and register-level instructions occupy different sweet spots of message size and compute cost — picking wrong can make an 'optimized' kernel slower than no overlap at all.
scenes.caption: Slide the message size and watch the copy engine's curve collapse below 256 MB while the TMA holds ~74–78% of peak from just 2 KB. This single chart explains why several prior systems lose to the do-nothing baseline on small matrices.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A log-x chart: message size (128 B to 256 MB) vs achieved fraction of NVLink peak bandwidth, three curves. Copy engine (a DMA unit driven by the CPU): reaches 81–82% of peak but only for contiguous messages ≥256 MB, collapsing steeply below. TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator, fired asynchronously by a single GPU thread): plateaus at ~74–78% from 2 KB up. Register load/store instructions: work at 128-byte granularity but are synchronous. A slider moves a vertical cursor; at the cursor, a side panel shows the 'compute tax' of saturating the link with each mechanism: 1 thread per transfer for TMA (~15 SMs total), ~76 of 132 SMs for register instructions, 0 SMs for the copy engine. A marker flags our workload's per-tile message (a 256×256 BF16 tile = 128 KB) — squarely where the copy engine fails and TMA thrives. Footnote flag on the register curve: 'only this mechanism can invoke the switch's arithmetic — hold that thought.' Predict-first: 'which curve should a fine-grained pipelined kernel use?'

animatedVariable.name: message size
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: A cursor sweeps the three bandwidth curves; the side panel updates each mechanism's achieved bandwidth and how many SMs it burns to saturate the link.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Copy engine: 81–82% of peak only at ≥256 MB messages; TMA: ~74–78% of peak from 2 KB, one thread per transfer; register instructions: 128 B granularity but ~76 SMs to saturate the link vs ~15 for TMA.
physicsChecks: The copy-engine curve is below the TMA curve everywhere left of ~256 MB and above it only at the far right.
The TMA curve is flat (~74–78%) from 2 KB onward.
The SM-cost panel always shows register instructions ≈76 SMs > TMA ≈15 SMs > copy engine 0 SMs.
The 128 KB per-tile marker sits in the region where the copy engine's achieved bandwidth is far below TMA's.

scenes.title: You can overlap inside every SM or dedicate whole SMs to communication — and each wins for different workloads
scenes.teaches: Intra-SM overlap (one thread per SM fires TMA transfers, ~64 ns on-chip sync) keeps all tensor cores busy; inter-SM overlap (a few SMs do only communication, ~832 ns sync through HBM) sacrifices compute but frees communication to batch, reuse cache, and reach the switch's arithmetic.
scenes.caption: Toggle the schedule on a map of all 132 SMs. Intra-SM wins GEMM + reduce-scatter (743.7 vs 618.1 TFLOP/s), but inter-SM wins GEMM + all-reduce by 3.6× (623.9 vs 172.3) — no single schedule wins both.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A grid of 132 squares, one per SM. Intra-SM mode: every square is mostly green (compute threads) with a thin orange stripe (one thread issuing TMA transfers); a sync annotation shows 'mbarrier, on-chip: ~64 ns'. Inter-SM mode: most squares fully green, a handful fully orange (dedicated communicator SMs); the sync path is drawn leaving the chip through HBM, annotated '~832 ns per synchronization'. Below the grid, two scoreboard rows show measured throughput for our two workload variants under each mode: GEMM+reduce-scatter (each GPU keeps its own slice of the sum) — intra 743.7 vs inter 618.1 TFLOP/s; GEMM+all-reduce (every GPU needs the full sum) — inter 623.9 vs intra 172.3 TFLOP/s. Predict-first: 'dedicating SMs throws away compute — can it ever win? Toggle and check the all-reduce row.' The reveal foreshadows the next scene: the inter-SM communicators can do something the intra-SM stripe cannot.

animatedVariable.name: scheduling strategy
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: SM squares repaint (thin orange stripes everywhere vs a few solid-orange SMs), the sync-latency annotation flips between 64 ns and 832 ns, and the scoreboard highlights which mode wins each workload.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Sync cost: ~64 ns intra-SM (mbarrier) vs ~832 ns inter-SM (through HBM); GEMM+RS: 743.7 vs 618.1 TFLOP/s favors intra-SM; GEMM+AR: 623.9 vs 172.3 TFLOP/s favors inter-SM.
physicsChecks: In inter-SM mode, solid-orange SMs do zero compute and the total green area strictly shrinks.
The sync annotation shows 64 ns in intra-SM mode and 832 ns in inter-SM mode, with the inter-SM path visibly routed off-chip through HBM.
The scoreboard shows opposite winners for the two workloads — neither mode dominates both rows.
The 3.6× gap (623.9 vs 172.3) appears on the all-reduce row exactly when toggling to inter-SM.

scenes.title: The switch between the GPUs can do the addition itself, cutting traffic roughly 8-fold
scenes.teaches: The NVSwitch fabric contains arithmetic units: multicast mappings fan one write out to all GPUs, and multimem instructions make the switch sum in-flight data — but only register-level instructions issued from dedicated SMs can invoke it.
scenes.caption: Here is why inter-SM won all-reduce by 3.6×. Toggle: naively, 7 partial sums queue at each GPU's single 450 GB/s inlet; with in-network reduction, the switch adds them in flight and delivers one result.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Eight GPU cards arranged around a central NVSwitch box. Naive mode: for one output tile of our matmul, animated packets stream from all 8 GPUs to every other GPU; at each destination, 7 incoming streams visibly queue at a single port labeled 'ingress: 450 GB/s' — the serialization is the visible cause. In-network mode: each GPU sends its partial tile once toward the switch; inside the switch, a small '+' unit merges the 8 streams and one summed packet fans out to all GPUs; a traffic meter shows total bytes on the wire dropping ~8×. An annotation ties back to the two previous scenes: 'invoked by multimem register instructions — the fine-grained mechanism from the bandwidth chart — issued by the dedicated communicator SMs from the schedule toggle.' Scoreboard: GEMM+all-reduce 3.62× faster with in-network reduction. Predict-first: 'the wire is usually a passive pipe — where else could the addition happen?'

animatedVariable.name: reduction strategy
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Packet flows reroute from 8-way all-pairs queues into a single fan-in at the switch's '+' unit; the traffic meter drops ~8× and the queue at each GPU's ingress port disappears.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: In-network reduction yields the paper's largest single win: 3.62× on GEMM + all-reduce; communication volume drops by roughly the GPU count (8×).
physicsChecks: In naive mode, the number of streams converging on each GPU equals 7 (N−1) and they visibly serialize at one 450 GB/s port.
In in-network mode, each GPU emits exactly one outbound stream and receives exactly one summed inbound stream.
The traffic meter reads ~8× lower in in-network mode with 8 GPUs.
The '+' arithmetic is drawn inside the switch, not on any GPU — the active-switch cause is visible.

scenes.title: Standard communication libraries pay a handshake-and-staging tax on every transfer
scenes.teaches: NCCL forces a sender-receiver handshake and stages data through intermediate buffers, and NVSHMEM does a memory load plus a thread-group sync per remote access; pre-exchanging destination addresses once enables direct one-way writes that skip all of it.
scenes.caption: Even a perfect schedule loses if each transfer drags this baggage. Step through one 128 KB tile transfer: NCCL takes four stops, ParallelKittens takes one — worth up to 1.79× on all-reduce and 4.5× lower latency.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Two horizontal swimlanes for the same job: 'move one 128 KB tile from GPU 0 to GPU 3'. Top lane (NCCL): four stepped stages — (1) sender-receiver handshake (two-way sync even though the write is one-sided), (2) copy into a small pre-allocated staging buffer, (3) transfer, (4) copy out of the receiver's staging buffer — each stage lighting up with its cost as the reader steps. A sidebar notes NVSHMEM's per-access tax: a global-memory load to fetch the peer's address plus a full thread-group synchronization. Bottom lane (ParallelKittens): a one-time setup step outside the loop — 'destination buffers mapped once via OS virtual memory; peer address held in a register' — then a single step: direct one-way write. A cumulative-latency counter runs above each lane. Scoreboard: up to 1.79× faster all-reduce, ~20 GB/s more bandwidth, up to 4.5× lower transfer latency. Predict-first: 'which stages are actually required to move the bytes?'

animatedVariable.name: transfer step
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Each NCCL stage lights in sequence and adds to its latency counter; the PK lane completes in one lit step, and the final counters show the gap.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Removing library overhead: up to 1.79× faster all-reduce, ~20 GB/s additional bandwidth, up to 4.5× lower transfer latency versus NCCL/NVSHMEM paths.
physicsChecks: The NCCL lane always shows strictly more stages (4) than the PK lane (1), and its latency counter ends higher.
The PK lane's address-exchange step is drawn outside the transfer loop — a one-time cost, not per-transfer.
The handshake stage shows arrows in both directions (two-way sync) while the PK write shows one direction only.
Extra copies appear only in the NCCL lane (staging buffers on both ends).

scenes.title: The whole design space compresses into eight primitives and one four-role template
scenes.teaches: Tiles plus a Parallel Global Layout, eight primitives (two async P2P stores, two switch-accelerated reductions, four barriers), and a loader/consumer/storer/communicator template express every technique from the previous five scenes in under 50 added lines per kernel.
scenes.caption: Everything we built — TMA overlap, dedicated communicator SMs, switch reduction, one-way writes — snaps into one template. Toggle the roles and watch our matmul's kernel assemble from eight named parts.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Left: the API surface as a labeled card deck — data types: 'tile' (16×16 to ~256×256 blocks matched to tensor-core layouts) and 'PGL: Parallel Global Layout, identically-shaped buffers replicated across all GPUs'; the eight primitives in three groups: async P2P (store_async, store_add_async — the TMA path from the bandwidth chart), switch-accelerated (reduce, all_reduce — the multimem path from the switch scene), barriers (signal, signal_all, wait, barrier). Right: the SM grid from the schedule scene, now with four color-coded roles — loader (stages tiles up the pyramid), consumer (tensor-core math), storer (fires TMA stores: intra-SM overlap), communicator (dedicated SMs: inter-SM overlap). A toggle switches our workload between GEMM+RS (communicator role off, storer active) and GEMM+AR (communicator SMs on, invoking all_reduce), showing that both winning configurations from earlier are instances of one template with an auto-tuned SM split. A line counter reads '<50 lines of added device code'. Predict-first: 'count the primitives you'd need for each workload — do you ever need one outside the eight?'

animatedVariable.name: workload configuration
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Role assignments repaint on the SM grid, the active primitives light up in the deck (store_async vs all_reduce plus barriers), and the SM split between compute and communication shifts.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Eight primitives total; each multi-GPU kernel needs under 50 lines of added device code; tile sizes span 16×16 to ~256×256.
physicsChecks: Exactly eight primitive cards exist, and every lit primitive in either configuration comes from that set.
The GEMM+RS configuration shows zero dedicated communicator SMs; the GEMM+AR configuration shows a nonzero block of them.
Each of the four roles maps back to a mechanism from an earlier scene (loader→pyramid, storer→TMA, communicator→multimem/inter-SM).
The line counter stays under 50 in both configurations.

scenes.title: Choosing the right mechanism per workload beats systems that hard-coded one choice
scenes.teaches: Because Flux, CUTLASS, Triton Distributed, and NCCL each fixed a single transfer mechanism or schedule, ParallelKittens beats them across four different parallelism workloads — and prior systems even lose to the non-overlapped baseline on small matrices.
scenes.caption: The scoreboard on 8×H100s: up to 2.33× over hand-tuned Flux, 4.08× over xDiT on ring attention, 1.22× over Comet, 1.07–5.63× over the Triton Distributed compiler. Shrink the matrix and watch the baselines fall below doing nothing at all.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A grouped bar chart, one group per workload — tensor-parallel GEMM (our running example), ring attention (a long input sequence split across GPUs, passed around a ring), MoE token dispatch (tokens scattered to specialist sub-networks on different GPUs), pure collectives — with bars for PK, the specialist system for that workload (Flux / xDiT / Comet / NCCL), Triton Distributed, and a gray dashed line for the non-overlapped baseline (compute then communicate, no tricks — the timeline from scene 3). Each unfamiliar workload gets a one-line inset definition at first appearance. A slider on matrix size for the GEMM group is the predict-first moment: 'shrink M and N — who dips below the gray line first?' Reveal: Flux and Triton Distributed sink under the baseline (their copy-engine-sized messages collapse, exactly as the bandwidth chart predicted) while PK stays above it. Each PK bar is annotated with which mechanism it chose, linking back to earlier scenes.

animatedVariable.name: matrix size for the tensor-parallel GEMM group
animatedVariable.range: small (message sizes in the copy engine's collapse zone) to large
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Baseline-system bars sink below the gray non-overlapped line at small sizes while the PK bar remains above it; at large sizes all systems rise but PK keeps its lead.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: 8×H100 results: up to 2.33× vs Flux on tensor-parallel GEMMs, 4.08× vs xDiT on ring attention, 1.22× vs Comet on MoE dispatch, 1.07–5.63× vs Triton Distributed.
physicsChecks: PK's bar is the tallest in every workload group at default settings.
At the small end of the matrix-size slider, at least one prior system's bar drops below the gray non-overlapped baseline while PK's does not.
The annotated mechanism on PK's bar differs across workload groups (TMA intra-SM vs multimem inter-SM vs fine-grained P2P), showing no single choice wins everywhere.
Speedup ratios read off the bars match the quoted 2.33×, 4.08×, 1.22× figures.

scenes.title: The compute–interconnect gap keeps widening, and the same 50-line kernels already win on the next GPU generation
scenes.teaches: Since interconnect bandwidth is not catching up to compute, overlap-based kernel design grows more valuable each generation — and PK's kernels ran unmodified on Blackwell B200s, hitting 1409 vs 960 TFLOP/s on GEMM + reduce-scatter.
scenes.caption: Flip our matmul from H100 to B200: the same unmodified kernel jumps to 1409 TFLOP/s against the baseline's 960. As the pipe falls further behind the math, hiding the pipe is the fix that compounds.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Two linked panels. Left: the hardware-trend chart from scene 3 extended with a shaded 'gap' region between the compute curve (×7.2 A100→B200) and the NVLink curve (×3), visibly widening rightward — the honest extrapolation: the paper's premise is that this gap will not close in hardware. Right: our running-example kernel as a code card stamped '<50 lines, unmodified', with a toggle between H100 and B200; a throughput dial shows PK's GEMM+RS at 1409 TFLOP/s vs the baseline's 960 on B200, alongside the H100 figures for scale. Closing annotation ties the throughline: the hiding condition \(K \geq sR/2B\) re-evaluates on B200's larger \(R\) — the threshold K rises, meaning overlap gets harder and the eight primitives matter more, not less. Predict-first: 'B200's tensor cores are faster — does the hiding threshold \(K\) go up or down?'

animatedVariable.name: GPU architecture
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The throughput dial jumps to 1409 vs 960 TFLOP/s, the code card stays byte-identical, and the recomputed hiding threshold \(K = sR/2B\) visibly increases with B200's larger R.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Same kernels unmodified on B200: 1409 TFLOP/s (PK) vs 960 TFLOP/s (baseline) on GEMM + reduce-scatter.
physicsChecks: The shaded gap between the compute and interconnect trend curves strictly widens from left to right.
The code card shows zero diff between H100 and B200 modes.
The recomputed threshold \(K = sR/2B\) increases when toggling to B200 (larger R, interconnect growing slower), consistent with the widening-gap panel.
The B200 dial reads 1409 vs 960 TFLOP/s, a ~1.47× gap, direction favoring PK on both architectures.
